ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry for samosa is used as the closest exact match for Punjabi Samosa, which is a regional variant.
ontology.foodex2_description: Samosa — deep-fried pastry with spiced potato and pea filling
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for samosa or Punjabi Samosa; not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Punjabi Samosa weighs approximately 80–120g per piece; a standard single serving is taken as 100g, equivalent to one medium-large samosa as commonly sold at street stalls and tea shops across North India.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Values for Punjabi Samosa are adapted from the samosa and fried snack entries in this publication, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for individual ingredient components (maida, potato, peas, oil) where direct samosa data is not individually listed. Macronutrient profile is consistent with the deep-fried pastry category in ICMR-NIN.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Punjabi Samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy with deep-fried refined-flour pastry products (e.g., white-flour pastry, GI ~70) and boiled potato (GI ~78), weighted by ingredient proportion. Refined flour (maida) and potato starch are the dominant carbohydrate contributors. This estimate is consistent with the ICMR-NIN assessment and is classified as high GI. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (70 × 28.0) / 100 = 19.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100g) and deep-fried preparation contributing to elevated fat intake
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Punjabi Samosa consumption due to its high sodium content of 350 mg per 100g; frequent intake may contribute to exceeding the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg and should be avoided as a regular snack.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) driven by refined flour (maida) and potato starch, with 28g carbohydrate and glycemic load of ~19.6 per 100g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Punjabi Samosa is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI of approximately 70 and high glycemic load of ~19.6 per serving, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion should be strictly limited to half a samosa and paired with a low-GI food.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with 14.5g total fat per 100g including saturated fat (~2.8g) and potential trans fat from repeated frying oil use
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Punjabi Samosa due to its high total fat content from deep-frying, elevated sodium, and potential trans fat formation during commercial frying, all of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried foods and nightshade vegetables (potatoes) may promote inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their personal response to Punjabi Samosa, as deep-fried foods and potato (a nightshade) may exacerbate inflammatory symptoms in some patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Punjabi Samosa is the most iconic and widely recognized regional variant of the Indian samosa, originating from the Punjab region of North India. It is distinguished by its large size, thick and flaky pastry shell made from all-purpose flour (maida) mixed with carom seeds (ajwain) and fat, which is deep-fried in oil until golden and crisp. The filling consists of spiced mashed potatoes and green peas seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, dried mango powder (amchur), and green chillies. The result is a snack with a crunchy exterior and a soft, aromatic, savory interior. Punjabi Samosa is a quintessential street food and tea-time snack across India, commonly served with tamarind chutney and mint-coriander chutney. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 260 kcal, 4.5g protein, 14.5g fat, and 28g carbohydrate, with 350mg sodium. It has a high glycemic index (~70) due to refined flour and potato starch, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is vegetarian and vegan but contains gluten. The dish is culturally significant during festivals, weddings, and everyday street food culture across North India and the Indian diaspora globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Punjabi Samosa?
What is the GI of samosa?
How much fat is in a samosa?
Can I eat samosa if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between Punjabi samosa and regular samosa?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN is the approved primary source for Indian dishes and supports the macronutrient profile. However, no single direct samosa entry with full micronutrient breakdown was available; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried snack category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — Punjabi Samosa is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in this vegan preparation. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — no animal or fortified ingredients are present in the traditional recipe. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a vegan fried snack with no animal fat or egg in the traditional Punjabi preparation. 2. water_g is set to -1: detailed moisture content for Punjabi Samosa as a composite deep-fried product is not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation; moisture data for this composite fried snack is unavailable from approved sources as of 2024. ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central were consulted and neither provides a direct moisture value for Punjabi Samosa or an equivalent composite deep-fried flour-and-potato pastry entry. 3. Saturated fat (2.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.2g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.5g), and trans_fat_g (0.1g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for vegetable oil used in deep-frying, proportioned to the fat content of the dish; these are approximations. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on refined flour pastry and potato starch components; no direct GI measurement for Punjabi Samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are applied at the samosa level as the closest defensible match; Punjabi Samosa as a distinct regional variant does not have its own separate ontology entry. 6. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries (all 100g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 100g.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Punjabi Samosa', a precise canonical English name. Hindi local name पंजाबी समोसा is present. Indian cuisine does not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil multilingual coverage under G6; G6 mandatory language rules apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes only.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (260), protein_g (4.5), fat_g (14.5), carbohydrate_g (28.0) all present and numeric. Source is ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, an approved primary source for Indian dishes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.2), sodium_mg (350.0), saturated_fat_g (2.8), sugar_g (1.5) all present as numeric values and plausible for a deep-fried flour-and-potato snack.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (70) present; gi_category ('high') present; glycemic_load_per_serving (19.6) present; gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Analogous estimate basis documented in source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311737 (samosa) used as exact match. FoodEx2 code A0CKP applied with description. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented; all are defensible.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,350 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains: 'Punjabi Samosa', 'पंजाबी समोसा', 'ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਮੋਸਾ', 'Samosa', 'Aloo Samosa', 'Potato Samosa', 'North Indian Samosa', 'Punjabi Samosa (Hindi: पंजाबी समोसा)', 'Punjabi Samosa (Punjabi: ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਮੋਸਾ)'. All values in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check passed.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Punjabi Samosa record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The sole failure from iteration 2 (g3_numeric_hygiene: water_g=-1 missing-data basis not documented) has been resolved by expanding the notes field to explicitly name ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved sources consulted, confirm that neither reports moisture content for this composite deep-fried preparation, and state the reason for unavailability. Core macros are complete and sourced from ICMR-NIN. Secondary nutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database basis, documented transparently. Ontology anchors are defensible. All three mandatory health conditions are present with clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias coverage is comprehensive and internally consistent. Record is output as _pass.json.
